title: Peer-led verbal outreach to improve colorectal cancer screening uptake: a randomized trial
abstract: Background: Colorectal cancer screening rates remain low in underserved communities. We evaluated a peer-led verbal education intervention against usual care. Methods: In this randomized controlled trial, community health workers delivered face-to-face education. Results: Screening uptake increased significantly in the intervention arm. Conclusions: Peer-led verbal outreach improves colorectal cancer screening.

title: Mailed print materials versus in-person navigation for colorectal cancer screening
abstract: Objective: To compare institutional print outreach with peer navigation. Design: Cluster randomized trial across 12 clinics. Participants received either mailed FIT kits with printed instructions or peer navigator support. Screening completion was the primary outcome. Print-only outreach was less effective than navigation.

title: Peer-led verbal outreach to improve colorectal cancer screening uptake: a randomized trial
abstract: Background: Colorectal cancer screening rates remain low in underserved communities. We evaluated a peer-led verbal education intervention against usual care. Methods: In this randomized controlled trial, community health workers delivered face-to-face education. Results: Screening uptake increased significantly in the intervention arm. Conclusions: Peer-led verbal outreach improves colorectal cancer screening.

title: A murine model of colorectal carcinogenesis under high-fat diet
abstract: We investigated tumor burden in mice fed a high-fat diet. Histopathology revealed increased adenoma formation. This animal study clarifies dietary mechanisms of colorectal carcinogenesis at the molecular level.

title: Editorial: The promise of community-based screening programs
abstract: This editorial reflects on recent advances in community outreach. No original data are presented. We call for more rigorous comparative effectiveness research.

title: Text-message reminders for colorectal cancer screening among Spanish-speaking patients
abstract: A pragmatic trial of SMS reminders. Bilingual text reminders modestly increased FIT return rates compared with no reminder. Digital outreach shows promise for screening promotion.

title: Community health worker interventions for cancer screening: a systematic review
abstract: We systematically reviewed the literature on community health worker interventions across breast, cervical, and colorectal cancer screening. Peer-led approaches consistently improved uptake. This review synthesizes 40 studies.

title: Cost-effectiveness of printed FIT-kit mailers in a safety-net health system
abstract: An economic evaluation of institutional print outreach. Mailed printed instructions with FIT kits were cost-effective relative to no outreach. Print-based institutional outreach can scale affordably.

title: Genomic biomarkers predicting adenoma recurrence after polypectomy
abstract: This laboratory study profiled tumor DNA to identify recurrence biomarkers. Next-generation sequencing revealed candidate markers. Findings are relevant to surveillance colonoscopy intervals, not to screening outreach.

title: Comparing peer educators and clinic brochures for colorectal screening in rural clinics
abstract: Methods: A head-to-head comparison of peer verbal education versus printed clinic brochures. Setting: Rural primary care. Results: Peer education outperformed brochures on screening completion at six months. Verbal peer outreach was superior to print in this rural sample.

title: Colonoscopy quality metrics in ambulatory surgery centers
abstract: We audited adenoma detection rates and cecal intubation across centers. This is a procedural quality study of colonoscopy performance and does not address patient outreach or screening invitation methods.

title: A patient navigation program to reduce colorectal screening disparities: verbal counseling component
abstract: Background: Disparities in colorectal cancer screening persist. Intervention: Trained navigators provided verbal counseling and telephone follow-up. Results: The verbal navigation program significantly raised screening rates among Black and Latino patients. Conclusion: Verbal navigation reduces disparities.
journal: Journal of Health Care for the Poor and Underserved
